text: The surgeon performs cystoscopy, introducing a cystoscope into the urethra. An infrared ureteric catheter is passed into each ureter using a guide wire. The catheter is six French and transparent, marked every five centimeters to ensure a fifteen-centimeter insertion using the Striker U kit.

The surgeon introduces a 15-centimeter infrared ureteric catheter into the right ureter, then removes it and introduces another catheter into the left ureter. Care is taken to avoid excessive bladder distension during the procedure. The guide wire is pulled out as the catheter is fully inserted into the left ureter.

The surgeon positions the camera on iris mode through the supra-umbilical port, following the baseball diamond concept for port placement. The first arc is positioned 18 centimeters from the target, and the second arc is 24 centimeters away. The right-hand port is designated for ligature or harmonic use, while the left-hand port is for graspers. The contralateral port is managed by the assistant.

A small stab wound is made at the camera point, and the surgeon lifts the abdominal wall to introduce the Veress needle. The needle is inserted until two click sounds are heard, followed by performing the irrigation test with a syringe, the suction test, and the hanging drop test to ensure proper placement.

The surgeon initiates insufflation using a striker nemoflator, monitoring the pressure as it increases to the preset level of fifteen. The actual pressure is observed at nine, and once 1.5 liters of gas is introduced, the flow rate is increased to three liters per minute. The abdominal distension is completed, and the surgeon enlarges the incision to 11 mm for the introduction of the optical trocar, which is carefully inserted.

The surgeon inserts an 11 mm trocar using a screwing motion, followed by the introduction of a telescope through the port. A 5 mm working port is placed on the right side, and another port is positioned 7.5 cm lateral to the first. A diagnostic laparoscopy is performed to inspect the uterus, confirming its condition before placing the uterine manipulator.

The surgeon uses a Mangeshkar uterine manipulator with a 3.5 cm colpotomizer to encircle the cervix. The tenaculum tip of the manipulator is introduced, taking bites on the anterior and posterior leaf of the cervix. The manipulator is carefully screwed in to ensure complete encirclement of the cervix, applying pressure with the colpotomizer.

The surgeon pushes the uterus towards the opposite shoulder tip and uses a ligature device to coagulate the round ligament, located four centimeters lateral to the uterus. Following the successful coagulation of the round ligament, the surgeon proceeds to the fallopian tube.

The surgeon uses a ligature to cook and cut the ovarian ligament approximately three centimeters away from the uterus, measuring with the open jaw of the ligature. The major salpings and major ovarian are also taken. The procedure continues with the broad ligament, moving blindly 2.5 centimeters over it. The right side is completed, and the surgeon proceeds to the left side, cooking and cutting the round ligament four centimeters lateral to the uterus.

The surgeon holds the fallopian tube and uses a ligature to desiccate and dissect it during the laparoscopic hysterectomy. The ureteric catheter is visible, blinking to indicate its position and prevent ureter damage. The uterus is pushed to the five o'clock position with a left-hand grasper, stretching the anterior leaf of the broad ligament. Using a right-hand harmonic device, the surgeon pushes the advantageous tissue down and begins cutting the peritoneum from left to right, while the assistant moves the uterus.

The uterus is manipulated from the five to seven o'clock position, appearing retroverted and nearly touching the right corner of the sacral promontory. The surgeon rotates the uterus, initially positioning it at five o'clock, then six o'clock, and finally seven o'clock. Using a harmonic device, the surgeon separates the tissue at each position.

The surgeon uses a suction tip to separate the bladder from the vaginal fascia visible over the colpotomizer, carefully pushing the bladder pillar laterally and upwards. The bladder shows a crisscross pattern of vessels. The posterior peritoneum is not taken, but coagulation is applied to the ascending uterine branch to prevent reverse bleeding. The uterine artery and vein are coagulated together without cutting.

The surgeon coagulates the uterine artery on the right side using a contralateral port, ensuring the ureter is visible to avoid injury. The uterine artery is desiccated but not cut initially. The process involves cooking both sides of the uterine artery before returning to cut it in a second attempt to ensure hemostasis.

The surgeon performs final coagulation and cutting of the uterine artery on the left side, ensuring complete proximal thrombosis of the vessel. The right ureter is visible, showing pulsation and blinking, while the left ureter is also visible. The char from the ligature is removed during the process. The ligature is then transferred to the right side for final coagulation and cutting of the vascular pedicle.
The surgeon coagulates and cuts the vascular bundle, removing both sides of the uterine artery using a ligature. The right side is completed, and the sine fascia is visible.

The surgeon completes the coagulation and cutting of the uterine vessels, securing both sides. The uterus appears pale and avascular. The colpotomy begins using the harmonic scalpel, with the ureteric stents visibly illuminated on either side. A few drops of blood are suctioned from the surgical field.

The colpotomizer is tightly fitted around the cervix to prevent vaginal shortening and avoid cutting the vaginal part of the uterosacral ligament. The surgeon performs anterior colpotomy using the tip of the harmonic scalpel, without closing its jaw, to carefully cut the tissue. The white Teflon colpotomizer is visible, providing a hard surface that resists cutting. The procedure progresses to posterior and lateral colpotomy, with the assistant applying slight force to facilitate cutting with minimal energy.

The surgeon addresses any oozing from the vaginal vault by using bipolar coagulation to prevent bleeding complications once the uterus is removed. The procedure involves performing a posterior colpotomy above the arc of the uterosacral ligament, preserving the vaginal part of the ligament by staying close to the cervix. The colpotomizer is tightly fitted to prevent vaginal wall collapse. The right lateral colpotomy is completed, freeing the uterus, which is then removed by the assistant.

The surgeon uses a tenaculum to hold the uterus as it is removed, preparing to pack the vagina with a sponge to prevent gas leakage. Anteromedial traction is applied to perform bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, using a ligature or harmonic device to coagulate and cut near the ovary. The assistant uses a stone-holding forcep to introduce the sponge into the vagina smoothly. The right salpingo-oophorectomy is completed with anteromedial traction, cutting perpendicular to the infundibulopelvic ligament, and the excised tissue is dropped into the vagina.

The assistant uses a stone holding forceps to introduce a curved needle with a number one vicryl suture through the vagina. The surgeon takes a bite on the uterosacral ligament at the corners, being careful to avoid pricking the uterine vessel.

The assistant holds the peritoneum anteriorly while the surgeon takes continuous suturing bites along the colpotomy wound, ensuring the bladder is not included. The suture tail is left longer, approximately three to four centimeters, and the surgeon progresses from one side to the opposite corner, locking the suture at the end. The surgeon takes bites over the utero sacral ligament and anterior vagina, interlocking at the corner to secure the suture.

The surgeon performs interlocking sutures on the vaginal vault, ensuring secure closure by locking each suture. The left hand holds the suture while the right hand runs over the vault, demonstrating a methodical approach to secure the closure.

The surgeon locks the suture and pulls the vaginal vault towards them, facilitating knot tying. The suture is returned to the opposite corner using the initial tail left earlier, and the final knot is tied securely at the corner. Care is taken to pull the suture rather than the needle to avoid accidental pricking.

The surgeon terminates the suturing process by pulling both sutures and tying a surgeon's knot with a double wrap followed by alternating wraps. The knot is tightened in the posterior fornix, and the tail of the suture is pulled through a 5mm cannula. The surgical field is cleaned with suction irrigation, revealing minimal blood loss and a well-maintained arc of the uterosacral ligament. The ureteric catheter is visible, blinking, as the telescope is removed.
